prompt: RSI (rapid sequence intubation) conceptually includes:

options.text: Near-simultaneous sedation and neuromuscular blockade to facilitate intubation in at-risk patients per protocol
Nasal cannula only without airway plan
Blind nasotracheal intubation of apneic patients as first line always
No preoxygenation ever

explanation: RSI requires preparation, preoxygenation, drugs, and post-intubation management; follows local ALS protocols/scope.

distractorExplanations.c: Apneic patients aren't good NT candidates.

prompt: Endotracheal tube placement confirmation should include:

options.text: Only watching the tube pass and nothing else
Waveform capnography plus clinical assessments (bilateral breath sounds, chest rise)
Absent ETCO2 accepted as success always
Epigastric sounds as the desired primary confirmation

explanation: Continuous waveform ETCO2 is the gold standard adjunct for confirming/monitoring ETT position.

prompt: A surgical cricothyrotomy is indicated when:

options.text: The patient has a minor nosebleed
SpO2 is 98% on room air with clear airway
Cannot intubate/cannot oxygenate scenarios require a front-of-neck airway per protocol
As first-line for all asthma patients

explanation: Cric is a rescue airway for failed airway algorithms—not routine first-line.

prompt: Positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) helps primarily by:

options.text: Decreasing oxygen saturation intentionally
Replacing the need for FiO2 forever
Guaranteeing no pneumothorax risk
Preventing alveolar collapse and improving oxygenation

explanation: PEEP improves FRC/oxygenation but can reduce venous return and risk barotrauma.

distractorExplanations.c: Pneumothorax risk can rise with high pressures.

prompt: Needle thoracostomy for tension pneumothorax is classically placed:

options.text: 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line or 4th/5th ICS mid/anterior axillary per current protocol updates
Through the umbilicus
Into the femoral vein
Subxiphoid only like pericardiocentesis landmark

explanation: Know both traditional MCL and lateral sites—follow current service protocol.

prompt: Waveform capnography in cardiac arrest can help:

options.text: Measure blood glucose
Indicate CPR quality and early ROSC (sudden ETCO2 rise)
Replace defibrillation pads
Diagnose fractures

explanation: Low ETCO2 suggests poor pulmonary blood flow/CPR; sustained rise may signal ROSC.

prompt: Apneic oxygenation during RSI often uses:

options.text: Turning off all oxygen
Only nitrous oxide
High-flow nasal oxygen during apnea to prolong safe apnea time
Encouraging breath-holding without O2

explanation: Passive oxygenation via NC can delay desaturation during laryngoscopy.

distractorExplanations.b: Nitrous not standard RSI apneic O2.

prompt: Laryngospasm management may include:

options.text: Oral fluids
Immediate exercise stress test
Removing all oxygen
High-quality BVM with jaw thrust/CPAP pressure and progressive advanced measures per protocol

explanation: Break spasm with airway maneuvers/pressure; drugs/paralysis per advanced protocols if needed.

prompt: The Mallampati classification estimates:

options.text: Relative difficulty of laryngoscopy based on oropharyngeal structures visible
Stroke severity (NIHSS)
Burn TBSA
GCS eye score only

explanation: Higher Mallampati scores correlate with potentially difficult airways—still imperfect.

prompt: Failed airway algorithm priority is:

options.text: Repeated attempts without oxygenation indefinitely
Oxygenation/ventilation—rescue devices and front-of-neck access if needed—not endless intubation attempts
Abandoning BVM forever
Focus only on IV starts

explanation: Limit attempts; maximize oxygenation with SGA/BVM/cric as indicated.

prompt: RSI (rapid sequence intubation) preparation should always include:

options.text: No suction because it slows the attempt
Only one laryngoscope blade with no backup
Equipment readiness for failed airway (BVM, suction, extraglottic/surgical backup) and confirmation plan
Skipping preoxygenation to save time

explanation: Airway attempts need oxygenation strategy and failure pathway ready.

prompt: Waveform capnography after intubation primarily helps confirm:

options.text: Exact hemoglobin level
Blood type
Intracranial pressure numerically
Tracheal placement and ongoing ventilation effectiveness/CO2 elimination

explanation: Persistent ETCO2 waveform supports tracheal tube placement and monitors ventilation.

prompt: A patient with status asthmaticus may benefit from which ventilation concept?

options.text: Allowing adequate expiratory time and avoiding excessive rates that cause auto-PEEP
Hyperventilating at 40 breaths/min continuously
Withholding oxygen to stimulate breathing
Forcing inspiration only without expiration

explanation: Obstructive disease needs time to exhale; stacked breaths cause hypotension/pneumothorax risk.

prompt: Needle cricothyrotomy/surgical airway is considered when:

options.text: The patient has a mild sore throat only
You cannot oxygenate/ventilate and cannot intubate (CICO) per protocol
SpO2 is 98% on room air with clear speech
As a first step before basic airway opening

explanation: Front-of-neck access is a last-resort failed airway intervention.

distractorExplanations.a: Mild URI is not an indication.
distractorExplanations.c: Adequate breathing needs no surgical airway.

prompt: Positive-pressure ventilation in a hypotensive patient can worsen BP because it:

options.text: Always increases preload dramatically
Removes all catecholamines from blood
Increases intrathoracic pressure and may reduce venous return
Converts asystole to sinus rhythm automatically

explanation: Excessive ventilation/PPv can drop preload—use appropriate rates/volumes and treat shock.

distractorExplanations.b: It does not strip catecholamines that way.
distractorExplanations.d: Ventilation alone does not pace asystole.

prompt: STEMI recognition on 12-lead focuses on:

options.text: ST elevation meeting criteria in anatomically contiguous leads (with clinical correlation)
Only sinus bradycardia always
Only peaked T waves from anxiety
Artifact as ST elevation diagnosis without scrutiny

explanation: Know anterior/inferior/lateral distributions; LBBB/paced rhythms complicate interpretation.

prompt: For adult VF/pVT, defibrillation energy should be:

options.text: 2 J always for adults
Per manufacturer biphasic recommended dose (often 120–200 J) then resume CPR
360 J synchronized cardioversion for VF
No shocks ever for VF

explanation: VF/pVT = unsynchronized defibrillation; synchronized cardioversion is for unstable tachycardias with pulse.

prompt: Atropine for symptomatic bradycardia adult dose commonly taught is:

options.text: 300 mg IV bolus like amiodarone VF dose
Aspirin 324 mg
1 mg IV every 3–5 minutes up to 3 mg (per current AHA-oriented protocols)
Adenosine 6 mg for bradycardia

explanation: If atropine fails, TCP/dopamine/epinephrine infusions per algorithm.

prompt: Adenosine is indicated for:

options.text: Irregular AF as first-line conversion always
Asystole
STEMI pain control
Stable regular narrow-complex SVT after vagal maneuvers (per protocol)

explanation: Adenosine briefly blocks AV node; caution in irregular wide-complex differentials.

distractorExplanations.c: STEMI pain often nitro/fentanyl per protocol.

prompt: ROSC care includes:

options.text: Airway optimization, BP support, 12-lead, and targeted temperature/neuro considerations per protocol
Immediate oral diet
Stopping all monitoring
Hyperventilation to ETCO2 of 10 mm Hg intentionally

explanation: Avoid hyperventilation; support perfusion and identify cause (H's/T's).

distractorExplanations.d: Low ETCO2 from hyperventilation harms ICP/perfusion.

prompt: Right ventricular infarction accompanying inferior MI suggests caution with:

options.text: Aspirin unconditionally never giving it
Nitroglycerin/preload reducers due to preload dependence
Oxygen when hypoxic
IV access

explanation: RV MI patients may become profoundly hypotensive with nitro; fluid support often needed.

distractorExplanations.a: Aspirin still usually given in ACS.

prompt: Wide-complex regular tachycardia in an unstable patient is treated with:

options.text: Only vagal maneuvers for hours
Oral beta blockers at home doses on scene only
Prompt synchronized cardioversion (or defibrillation if pulseless/degenerate)
Ignoring pulses

explanation: Unstable = shock; stable wide-complex needs expert interpretation/antiarrhythmics per protocol.

distractorExplanations.b: Home PO meds not EMS unstable care.

prompt: Amiodarone in cardiac arrest (VF/pVT refractory) adult dosing commonly taught includes:

options.text: 1 mg like atropine
6 mg like adenosine
324 mg chewed
300 mg IV/IO first dose, then 150 mg

explanation: Follow current ACLS drug sequence with CPR/defibrillation priority.

prompt: CPAP for acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema helps by:

options.text: Increasing intrathoracic pressure to reduce preload/afterload and improve oxygenation
Causing alveolar collapse intentionally
Replacing nitroglycerin always as contraindicated combo
Guaranteeing intubation is never needed

explanation: CPAP is a key ALS tool for CHF/flash edema when patient can cooperate.

distractorExplanations.c: Nitro often used with CPAP if BP allows.

prompt: Hyperkalemia ECG changes may include:

options.text: Delta waves of WPW exclusively
Peaked T waves, widened QRS, and sine-wave progression
U waves of hypokalemia exclusively as hyperK sign
Osborne J waves of hypothermia exclusively as hyperK

explanation: Severe hyperK is a life threat treated with calcium/stabilizers/shifting agents per protocol.

prompt: For stable narrow-complex SVT (per ACLS concepts), initial medication often considered is:

options.text: Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at max energy as first step if stable with a pulse
Oral digoxin crushed in the field always
Adenosine (when vagal maneuvers fail/are inappropriate), per current protocol
High-dose insulin as first-line SVT drug

explanation: Stable SVT algorithms use vagal maneuvers/adenosine; unstable patients may need synchronized cardioversion.

distractorExplanations.a: Unsynchronized defib is for VF/pulseless VT.
distractorExplanations.b: Oral digoxin is not typical field SVT first-line.
distractorExplanations.d: Insulin is for other toxicology/metabolic care.

prompt: Synchronized cardioversion is preferred over unsynchronized defibrillation when:

options.text: The monitor shows fine VF without pulse
Asystole is present
The patient is awake and stable with sinus rhythm
The patient has a pulse with an unstable tachyarrhythmia requiring shock

explanation: Synchronization avoids T-wave shock in organized rhythms with pulses; VF needs defib.

distractorExplanations.b: Asystole is not a shockable rhythm.

prompt: STEMI recognition on 12-lead should prompt:

options.text: Rapid transport/activation of PCI-capable pathways and ACS care bundle per protocol
Discharge home with advice to rest
Waiting for outpatient stress testing next month before EMS care
Giving all patients oral antibiotics

explanation: Time-to-reperfusion matters; prenotify STEMI systems of care.

prompt: Hyperkalemia ECG changes may include:

options.text: Delta waves pathognomonic only for hypokalemia
Peaked T waves and widened QRS progressing toward sine-wave/VT risk
U waves as the only possible finding always
Normal ECG guaranteed until cardiac arrest

explanation: Rising K+ alters repolarization/conduction—treat per protocol (calcium, shifts, etc.).

distractorExplanations.a: Delta waves relate to WPW patterns.

prompt: ROSC care priorities include:

options.text: Stopping all monitoring because the pulse returned
Hyperventilating to ETCO2 of 10 mmHg routinely
Support ABCs, optimize oxygenation/ventilation carefully, treat hypotension, and consider targeted temperature/PCI indications per protocol
Giving unrestricted oral fluids immediately in comatose patients

explanation: Post-arrest care prevents secondary brain/heart injury.

prompt: Permissive hypotension in penetrating trauma (select protocols) aims to:

options.text: Avoid popping clots with excessive fluids before surgical hemorrhage control
Raise SBP to 200 mm Hg in all trauma
Withhold all TXA/blood products always
Ignore airway

explanation: Balanced resuscitation/blood products increasingly preferred over large crystalloid dumps.

prompt: TXA (tranexamic acid) in trauma is used to:

options.text: Dissolve clots like tPA in STEMI
Inhibit fibrinolysis and reduce hemorrhage mortality when given early per protocol
Treat hypoglycemia
Reverse opioid OD

explanation: CRASH-2 timing matters—early administration; not a fibrinolytic.

prompt: Neurogenic shock features often include:

options.text: Tachycardia with cold clammy skin always like classic hypovolemia only
Fever and petechiae only
Hypotension with bradycardia and warm dry skin after spinal cord injury
Wheezing and hives only

explanation: Loss of sympathetic tone distinguishes neurogenic from hypovolemic shock patterns.

prompt: Status epilepticus treatment priority after airway/BVM is often:

options.text: Oral glucose gel forced during convulsions
Aspirin
Nitroglycerin
Benzodiazepines per protocol (e.g., midazolam/lorazepam/diazepam)

explanation: Stop seizure activity pharmacologically while protecting airway.

prompt: DKA typically presents with:

options.text: Hyperglycemia, dehydration, and Kussmaul respirations with metabolic acidosis
Hypoglycemia and cool diaphoresis only
Normal glucose with isolated stroke symptoms only
Opioid pinpoint pupils only

explanation: Support ABC, fluids per protocol, and transport; watch potassium shifts with treatment.

prompt: Anaphylactic shock first-line drug is:

options.text: Oral diphenhydramine alone as sufficient monotherapy
Epinephrine IM (and IV/IO escalation per protocol if refractory)
Albuterol only without epi
Furosemide

explanation: Epi treats vasodilation/bronchospasm; antihistamines/steroids are adjuncts.

prompt: Cushing's reflex suggests:

options.text: Hypovolemic shock
Isolated hypoglycemia
Rising ICP with hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations
Asthma mild exacerbation

explanation: Late herniation sign—optimize oxygenation/ventilation and rapid transport/neurosurgical destination.

distractorExplanations.a: Hypovolemia is opposite BP/HR pattern often.

prompt: Burn fluid resuscitation formulas (e.g., Parkland concepts) calculate fluids using:

options.text: Only GCS
Only pupil size
Favorite IV color
TBSA burned and patient weight (and timing from injury)

explanation: Know Rule of Nines/Lund-Browder ideas; avoid over/under resuscitation; airway inhalation injury risks.

prompt: Sepsis alert criteria emphasize:

options.text: Infection suspicion plus vital sign/perfusion abnormalities needing early fluids/cultures/antibiotics at hospital
Only chronic stable hypertension
Isolated ankle sprain
Well-controlled hypothyroidism exclusively

explanation: Prehospital sepsis recognition speeds door-to-treatment.

prompt: Tension pneumothorax vs cardiac tamponade: tamponade more often shows:

options.text: Unilateral absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation as the hallmark of tamponade
Muffled heart sounds, JVD, and hypotension (Beck's triad) without unilateral absent breath sounds dominance
Wheezing bilaterally only
Hyperglycemia

explanation: Both cause obstructive shock; differentiate by lung findings vs muffled tones/equal breath sounds.

prompt: Tranexamic acid (TXA) in trauma (when used per protocol) is intended to:

options.text: Reverse warfarin like PCC/vitamin K
Replace blood typing
Reduce bleeding by inhibiting fibrinolysis in selected hemorrhagic patients within time windows
Treat anaphylaxis as epinephrine does

explanation: TXA is antifibrinolytic adjunct—not a warfarin reversal agent or epinephrine substitute.

prompt: A burn patient with suspected inhalation injury may show:

options.text: Only ankle edema without respiratory signs
Guaranteed normal airway for 24 hours
Isolated urinary retention as the hallmark
Hoarseness, stridor, facial burns, or carbonaceous sputum—airway may worsen

explanation: Early airway assessment/management is critical because edema progresses.

distractorExplanations.c: Urinary retention is not the classic sign.

prompt: For suspected tension pneumothorax with hemodynamic collapse, paramedic management may include:

options.text: Needle thoracostomy/decompression in the correct site per protocol plus support ABCs
High-dose furosemide as first-line
Oral antibiotics only
Trendelenburg and observation without airway/oxygen support

explanation: Tension physiology needs decompression and ventilation/oxygenation support.

distractorExplanations.b: Diuretics are for fluid overload, not tension PTX.

prompt: Sepsis alert criteria often include:

options.text: Isolated well-controlled seasonal allergies
Suspected infection plus signs of hypoperfusion/organ dysfunction (e.g., hypotension, altered mentation, elevated lactate when available)
Stable vitals with minor bruise only
Hyperglycemia alone without infection concern always

explanation: Early recognition and fluids/pressors/antibiotics (hospital) improve outcomes—prenotify when protocols exist.

distractorExplanations.c: Minor trauma alone is not sepsis.

prompt: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose toxicity may feature:

options.text: Pathognomonic opioid pinpoint pupils reversed only by flumazenil
Isolated hypoglycemia treated only with glucagon always
Wide-complex dysrhythmias and seizures—sodium bicarbonate per protocol for wide QRS/toxicity
Mandatory calcium channel blocker infusion as first antidote

explanation: TCA sodium-channel blockade → wide QRS; bicarb is a key therapy; flumazenil is for benzos (caution).

distractorExplanations.a: Opioid toxidrome differs; flumazenil is not TCA antidote.
distractorExplanations.b: TCA tox is not primarily a glucagon hypoglycemia pathway.
distractorExplanations.d: CCB infusion is not the TCA antidote.

prompt: START triage 'RPM' stands for:

options.text: Respirations, Perfusion, Mental status
Radio, Police, Medic
Reflexes, Pain, Motor only as START
Risk, Preference, Money

explanation: START categories: Immediate, Delayed, Minor, Expectant based on RPM walking filter.

prompt: ICS span of control ideally is about:

options.text: 50–100 direct reports ideally
3–7 subordinates per supervisor (often ~5)
Zero communication
Only one incident type worldwide

explanation: Manageable span of control keeps command effective.

prompt: Landing zone for air medical should generally be:

options.text: Under power lines for reference
On a steep rocky slope preferred
Approximately 100 × 100 feet (day) of level, clear area with secure hazards, per local guidance
Marked with loose blankets that fly into rotors

explanation: Secure debris, mark corners, and communicate with crew; night LZ larger often.

prompt: Pediatric assessment triangle components are:

options.text: BP, ECG, and glucose only
Only fontanelle and teeth
APGAR only for all ages
Appearance, work of breathing, and circulation to skin

explanation: PAT gives rapid 'sick vs not sick' without equipment.

prompt: Neonatal resuscitation initial priority after warmth/position/stimulate is:

options.text: Effective ventilation if HR remains low/apneic
Immediate epinephrine before any PPV always
Chest compressions before any breaths always
Oral feeding

explanation: PPV is the most important neonatal action; compressions if HR <60 after ventilation.

prompt: OB emergency: prolapsed cord management includes:

options.text: Clamping and cutting the cord immediately on scene always before hospital
Elevate presenting part off the cord, maternal positioning, and rapid transport without pushing cord back in roughly
Having mother walk vigorously
Ignoring fetal bradycardia

explanation: Keep cord moist/protected and maintain elevation of the presenting part per protocol.

distractorExplanations.a: Don't cut as first step routinely.

prompt: Behavioral emergency safety priority is:

options.text: Always tackling alone immediately
Ignoring weapon clues
Scene safety and de-escalation; restrain only as needed for safety with adequate personnel
Agreeing to be locked in a room without egress

explanation: Request law enforcement when needed; chemical/physical restraint per protocol/law.

prompt: HAZMAT operations for EMS generally emphasize:

options.text: Running into vapor clouds without SCBA to grab victims quickly always
Using street clothes as Level A
Ignoring placards
Operating in cold zone with decontaminated patients; don't enter hot zone without training/PPE

explanation: Recognize, isolate, protect yourself, and await specialists.

distractorExplanations.b: Level A is fully encapsulating specialized.

prompt: Due regard while driving emergently means:

options.text: Exercising caution for public safety despite exemptions; you can still be liable for reckless driving
Absolute right-of-way at any speed
No need for seat belts in the back
Ignoring school bus signals always

explanation: Lights/sirens request right-of-way—they don't guarantee it.

prompt: For suspected child abuse, EMS should:

options.text: Confront and detain parents forcibly as EMS role always
Treat injuries, transport as needed, document objectively, and report per mandatory reporting laws
Ignore injuries if a parent refuses politely in a life threat
Post details on social media

explanation: Report suspicions to authorities per law; medical care and objective charting are key.

distractorExplanations.c: Life threats: treat/transport under implied consent/mandates.

prompt: When operating on an MCI with START triage, a walking patient is generally tagged:

options.text: Immediate red automatically
Expectant black automatically
Minor (green) unless later reassessment changes priority
Dead without assessment

explanation: Ambulatory patients are initially minor; re-triage as needed.

prompt: Neonatal resuscitation prioritizes:

options.text: Immediate adult-dose epinephrine IO as first step always
Oral feeding before drying
Ignoring temperature
Warming, airway positioning/clearing, and effective ventilation before extensive medication focus

explanation: Most newborns respond to airway/ventilation and thermal care; meds follow algorithms.

distractorExplanations.a: Meds come after ventilation steps when indicated.
distractorExplanations.b: Feeding is not NRP first step.

prompt: A pregnant patient of ~28 weeks in cardiac arrest should receive:

options.text: High-quality CPR with left uterine displacement and prioritization of maternal resuscitation/airway
No chest compressions to protect the fetus
Only transport without CPR if arrest continues
Abdominal thrusts instead of compressions

explanation: Maternal circulation is fetal circulation—CPR with uterine displacement is key; peri-mortem cesarean is a hospital consideration.

distractorExplanations.d: Thrusts are not a substitute for CPR.

prompt: Landing zone safety for helicopter EMS includes:

options.text: Running under the tail rotor to save time
Securing hazards, marking a clear zone, and protecting eyes/ears from rotor wash—approach only when signaled
Approaching from the uphill side of a slope near blades
Carrying tall IV poles upright into the rotor disk

explanation: Rotor hazards kill—controlled approach after crew signals.

distractorExplanations.c: Uphill approach brings you into blades.

prompt: Which statement about documentation in EMS is correct?

options.text: Alter vitals later to match expected norms
Omit refusals to avoid paperwork
The PCR should be accurate, timely, and complete enough to support continuity of care and legal review
Copy another patient's narrative to save time

explanation: Truthful documentation protects patients and providers.
